---
title: "Getting Started with Claude Code"
subtitle: "A practical guide for IPA Colombia Research Analysts"
author: "IPA Colombia MEL Team"
date: today
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 2
    toc-location: left
    number-sections: false
    embed-resources: true
    theme:
      light: cosmo
      dark: darkly
    smooth-scroll: true
    code-copy: true
    link-external-newwindow: true
    css: styles.css
execute:
  echo: false
  eval: false
---

```{=html}
<div class="hero-banner">
  <div class="hero-content">
    <p class="hero-eyebrow">IPA Colombia &middot; 50–75 min</p>
    <h1 class="hero-title">Learn Claude Code<br>for Stata Research</h1>
    <p class="hero-subtitle">
      Go from zero to productive with an AI coding assistant.<br>
      No prior experience with VS Code or Claude Code required.
    </p>
    <div class="hero-badges">
      <span class="badge-item">📖 Reading + 2 Exercises</span>
      <span class="badge-item">🔒 PII Safety Rules</span>
      <span class="badge-item">🎯 Stata-Focused</span>
    </div>
  </div>
</div>
```

# Before You Start {#sec-prereqs}

This guide is designed for research analysts who are proficient in Stata but have not used Visual Studio Code (VS Code) or Claude Code before. It progresses from reading to doing: Sections 1 through 5 build understanding, Section 6 teaches prompting, and Sections 7 and 8 are hands-on exercises.

:::: {.callout-note appearance="simple"}
## Prerequisites

::::: {.grid}
:::: {.g-col-6}
- Stata 17 or later installed and licensed
- A project repository cloned from GitHub
::::
:::: {.g-col-6}
- Node.js 18+ installed ([download](https://nodejs.org/))
- An Anthropic API key **or** a Claude Max/Pro account
::::
:::::
::::

---

# VS Code in Five Minutes {#sec-vscode}

Most research analysts switch between the Stata graphical user interface (GUI), a file browser, and a terminal window throughout the day. VS Code puts all three into one screen. It is a free, open-source code editor maintained by Microsoft and the environment where Claude Code runs.

## The Four Panels

::::: {.grid}
:::: {.g-col-6}
:::{.card}
### Sidebar
Shows your project folder structure. Toggle it with `Ctrl+B`. Click any file to open it in the editor.
:::
::::
:::: {.g-col-6}
:::{.card}
### Editor
The main area where files open as tabs. Split the view to compare two files side by side.
:::
::::
:::: {.g-col-6}
:::{.card}
### Terminal
A command line built into VS Code. This is where Claude Code runs. Toggle it with `` Ctrl+` ``.
:::
::::
:::: {.g-col-6}
:::{.card}
### Extensions
A marketplace for add-ons. Open with `Ctrl+Shift+X`. You will install three extensions below.
:::
::::
:::::

## Three Essential Actions

::: {.panel-tabset}
### Windows

| Action | Shortcut |
|--------|----------|
| Open a folder | `Ctrl+K`, then `Ctrl+O` |
| Open the terminal | `` Ctrl+` `` |
| Search across all files | `Ctrl+Shift+F` |

### macOS

| Action | Shortcut |
|--------|----------|
| Open a folder | `Cmd+O` |
| Open the terminal | `` Ctrl+` `` |
| Search across all files | `Cmd+Shift+F` |
:::

## Extensions to Install

Open the VS Code terminal and run these one at a time:

```bash
code --install-extension kylebarron.stata-enhanced
code --install-extension anthropic.claude-code
code --install-extension eamodio.gitlens
```

::: {.callout-tip appearance="simple"}
Do not worry about mastering VS Code now. You will learn it by using it during the exercises. The three actions above are enough.
:::

---

# What Is Claude Code? {#sec-what-is-cc}

Claude Code is an AI coding assistant built by Anthropic. It runs inside the VS Code terminal as a command-line tool. Unlike browser-based chatbots, Claude Code reads the files in your project folder, understands the structure, and can write or edit code directly on your machine.

::::: {.grid}
:::: {.g-col-4}
:::{.card}
### 🗂️ Project Context
Reads your files automatically. It sees do-files, configs, and docs. No copy-pasting into a chat window.
:::
::::
:::: {.g-col-4}
:::{.card}
### ✏️ Direct Editing
Creates, modifies, and deletes files in your project. It asks for approval before making changes.
:::
::::
:::: {.g-col-4}
:::{.card}
### 📋 Instructions
Follows rules in a `CLAUDE.md` file at your project root. Coding standards, conventions, and guardrails.
:::
::::
:::::

### What It Can Do for Stata Work

- Write new do-files following Innovations for Poverty Action (IPA) and DIME Analytics standards
- Debug errors by reading Stata log files
- Generate LaTeX tables using `esttab` or `outreg2`
- Explain existing code in plain language

### What It Cannot Do

- Access or read data that contains personally identifiable information (PII)
- Replace your judgment on research design or methodology
- Run Stata by itself (it uses project commands like `just stata-run`)
- Access Box, email, or systems outside your machine

---

# Installation and First Run {#sec-install}

The setup takes about five minutes. There are two ways to authenticate Claude Code: with an API key (pay per use) or with a Claude Max/Pro account (monthly subscription). Choose the one that applies to you.

## Step 1: Install Claude Code

Open the VS Code terminal (`` Ctrl+` ``) and run:

```bash
npm install -g @anthropic-ai/claude-code
```

::: {.callout-note appearance="simple"}
If you see a permissions error on Windows, close VS Code and reopen it as Administrator. On macOS, prefix with `sudo`.
:::

## Step 2: Authenticate

::: {.panel-tabset}

### Option A: API Key

An API key charges per use. This is the standard setup for IPA projects where the team shares a billing account.

Set the key as an environment variable:

::: {.panel-tabset}
#### Windows (PowerShell)

```powershell
[System.Environment]::SetEnvironmentVariable("ANTHROPIC_API_KEY", "sk-ant-your-key-here", "User")
```

Restart VS Code after running this command.

#### macOS / Linux

Add to `~/.zshrc` or `~/.bashrc`:

```bash
export ANTHROPIC_API_KEY="sk-ant-your-key-here"
```

Then restart your terminal.
:::

### Option B: Claude Account (Max/Pro)

If you have a Claude Max or Pro subscription, you can use Claude Code without a separate API key. This authenticates through your personal Claude account.

Launch Claude Code (Step 3 below) and follow the browser login prompt. Claude Code will open a browser window where you sign in with your Claude account credentials.

No environment variable is needed. Claude Code stores the session token locally after the first login.
:::

::: {.callout-warning appearance="simple"}
**Keep credentials private.** Never paste your API key into a shared document, Slack message, or Git commit. If using a Claude account, do not share your login session.
:::

## Step 3: Launch and Verify

Navigate to your project folder and start Claude Code:

```bash
cd /path/to/your/project
claude
```

Claude Code reads the `CLAUDE.md` file on startup. To verify it works, type:

```
What project is this?
```

It should describe your project based on the `CLAUDE.md` contents. For the SpC project, it mentions Sanar para Crecer 3.0 (SPC), ICBF, and the Monitoring, Evaluation, and Learning (MEL) pipeline.

---

# How Claude Code Knows Your Project {#sec-project-context}

Claude Code does not start from zero each time. Three mechanisms give it persistent knowledge of your project.

## CLAUDE.md: The Project Briefing

Think of `CLAUDE.md` as the briefing you would write for a new research analyst on their first day. It tells Claude Code what the project is, what rules to follow, and where to find things.

```yaml
# CLAUDE.md — SpC Project

## What This Project Is
SpC 3.0 = Sanar para Crecer 3.0 (HtG)
A large-scale caregiver education program implemented by ICBF
in Colombia. The program includes an embedded RCT.

## Project-Specific Rules
- PII: Raw data contains PII — never read or display raw data files.
- Do not use cap / capture except for cap mkdir.
- Path globals are defined in 0_paths.do. Never redefine them.
```

This file is checked into Git, so every team member shares the same instructions.

## Skills: The Reference Manuals

The `.claude/skills/` directory contains specialized instruction sets. Each skill is a markdown file with rules, examples, and conventions. Two key skills:

::::: {.grid}
:::: {.g-col-6}
:::{.card}
### Stata Skill
IPA/DIME coding standards, do-file headers, path conventions, missing value handling, the four-stage data flow.
:::
::::
:::: {.g-col-6}
:::{.card}
### IPA Writing Skill
Style rules for all IPA content: paragraph structure, active voice, acronym conventions, citation format.
:::
::::
:::::

Claude Code loads the relevant skill automatically. Ask it to write a do-file and it applies the Stata skill. Ask it to draft text and it applies writing guidelines.

**Mental model:** `CLAUDE.md` is the briefing for a new research analyst. Skills are the reference manuals on their desk.

## Settings and Permissions

The `.claude/settings.json` file controls which commands Claude Code can run without asking. In the SpC project, Stata commands through the `just` runner are pre-approved.

::: {.callout-note appearance="simple"}
You do not need to modify any of these files. They are maintained by the project lead. Understanding that they exist explains why Claude Code "already knows" your conventions.
:::

---

# Security and PII Rules {#sec-security}

:::{.callout-danger appearance="simple"}
**This is the most important section in the guide.** Read it carefully before using Claude Code on any project with human subjects data.
:::

IPA projects handle data with personally identifiable information: names, national identification numbers (cedula), addresses, and phone numbers. Claude Code can read any file in your VS Code workspace and sends your messages to external servers for processing.

## The Four Rules

::::: {.grid}
:::: {.g-col-6}
:::{.card .card-danger}
### Rule 1: No PII in Prompts
Do not type or paste names, cedula numbers, addresses, or phone numbers into Claude Code.
:::
::::
:::: {.g-col-6}
:::{.card .card-danger}
### Rule 2: No Raw Data Files
Never ask Claude Code to open files in `data/raw/` or the encrypted vault (D: drive). Work only with do-files, logs, and de-identified outputs.
:::
::::
:::: {.g-col-6}
:::{.card .card-danger}
### Rule 3: No Individual-Level Output
Do not ask Claude Code to display rows of data. Even de-identified data can become re-identifiable. Ask it to write code that produces summaries instead.
:::
::::
:::: {.g-col-6}
:::{.card .card-danger}
### Rule 4: Review Before Committing
Check every file Claude Code creates or modifies. Confirm no PII leaked into comments, labels, or hard-coded values.
:::
::::
:::::

## Safe vs. Unsafe: Quick Test

> Before sending a prompt, ask: "Does this require Claude Code to see individual-level data or personal details?" If yes, rephrase to focus on the **code**, not the **data**.

| Scenario | Verdict |
|----------|---------|
| "Write a do-file that cleans survey data following this structure" | ✅ Safe |
| "Clean this survey data" | ❌ Unsafe — Claude Code would read PII |
| "Why is my merge failing? Here is the log" | ✅ Safe — logs have aggregates |
| "Show me the first 20 rows of the baseline dataset" | ❌ Unsafe — individual-level data |
| "Debug this r(111) error in my do-file" | ✅ Safe — reads code, not data |

---

# Prompt Engineering for Stata {#sec-prompts}

Five patterns for writing effective prompts. Each shows a weak and a strong version.

## 1. Describe the Output, Not the Steps

::: {.panel-tabset}
### ❌ Weak

```
Use regress, then outreg2, then add a title, then save as .tex
```

### ✅ Strong

```
Run an ITT regression of FFMQ on treatment with strata fixed effects
and SEs clustered at the UDS level. Export to LaTeX using the outreg2
conventions in CLAUDE_DELIVERABLES_GUIDE.md.
```
:::

## 2. Reference Project Conventions

::: {.panel-tabset}
### ❌ Weak

```
Make a nice table with the results
```

### ✅ Strong

```
Export using the esttab pattern in CLAUDE_DELIVERABLES_GUIDE.md.
Match the column structure of the attrition tables in outputs/tables/.
```
:::

## 3. Provide Context About Errors

::: {.panel-tabset}
### ❌ Weak

```
I got r(111), please fix
```

### ✅ Strong

```
I ran `just stata-script mel/2_AttritionAnalysis` and got r(111).
Here are the last 30 lines of the log:

[paste log excerpt]

The error is on the line referencing `treatment_status`.
I think the variable was renamed upstream.
```
:::

## 4. Ask for Explanations

::: {.panel-tabset}
### ❌ Weak

```
Write the merge command
```

### ✅ Strong

```
Write the merge command and explain why you chose 1:1 vs m:1.
Also explain what the assert results mean.
```
:::

## 5. Iterate, Do Not Start Over

::: {.panel-tabset}
### ❌ Weak

```
[rewrites the entire prompt from scratch]
```

### ✅ Strong

```
The table looks right. Add baseline controls as a second column
and include the F-test for joint significance at the bottom.
```
:::

Claude Code remembers the conversation. Build on its output rather than starting over.

---

# Exercise 1: Debug a Stata Error {#sec-exercise1}

:::{.exercise-banner}
**Hands-on Exercise** · 10–15 minutes
:::

### The Scenario

You ran an attrition analysis script and the log shows an error:

```stata
. use "$data/clean/masterMUESTRA_final_RCT.dta", clear

. gen attrited = (endline_survey == 0)

. regress attrited treatment_status i.strata, vce(cluster cod_uds)
variable treatment_status not found
r(111);

end of do-file
r(111);
```

### Your Task

1. Open Claude Code in your project folder (run `claude` in the terminal).
2. Paste the log and ask: *"I got this r(111) error running my attrition analysis. What is wrong and how do I fix it?"*
3. Read the diagnosis. Does it identify `r(111)` as "variable not found"?
4. Follow up: *"Check what treatment-related variables exist in the do-files."*
5. Evaluate the proposed fix.

### What to Look For

- Does Claude Code identify the correct error type?
- Does it suggest checking existing do-files for the real variable name?
- Does the fix follow conventions (no `capture`, proper path globals)?

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true" appearance="simple"}
## Reveal Answer

The variable is named `treatment` (or `treat`), not `treatment_status`. This happens when variable names change during cleaning but downstream scripts are not updated. The fix is a single-line change, not a workaround with `capture` or `rename`.
:::

---

# Exercise 2: Write a Summary Statistics Do-File {#sec-exercise2}

:::{.exercise-banner}
**Hands-on Exercise** · 15–20 minutes
:::

### The Scenario

Your project lead asks for a summary statistics table of early childhood development (ECD) worker outcomes at endline, by treatment group.

### Step 1: Write the Prompt

Use this specification to write your prompt (remember Pattern 1: describe the output):

::::: {.grid}
:::: {.g-col-6}
**Variables:**

- `ffmq_total` (mindfulness)
- `dass_stress` (stress)
- `dass_anxiety` (anxiety)
- `dass_depression` (depression)
- `psi_total` (parenting stress)
::::
:::: {.g-col-6}
**Requirements:**

- Breakdown by treatment arm (0 = control, 1 = treatment)
- Mean, SD, N, and t-test p-value
- LaTeX output via `esttab`
- Follow `CLAUDE_DELIVERABLES_GUIDE.md`
::::
:::::

::: {.callout-note appearance="simple"}
**Hint:** Tell Claude Code what table you need and reference the deliverables guide. You do not need to specify each Stata command.
:::

### Step 2: Review the Output

Check the generated do-file against this list:

- [ ] Proper header (project, purpose, author, date)
- [ ] Path globals (`$data`, `$tables`) instead of hard-coded paths
- [ ] No `capture` (except `cap mkdir`)
- [ ] Extended missing values handled (`.d`, `.n`, `.r`, `.s`)
- [ ] Clean `esttab` call producing LaTeX output
- [ ] Output saved to the `$tables` directory

### Step 3: Iterate

Ask Claude Code for two modifications:

1. *"Add a column with the treatment-control difference and the t-test p-value."*
2. *"Exclude `.d` (do not know) and `.r` (refused) from calculations but keep `.n` (not applicable) observations."*

### Step 4: Evaluate

Did Claude Code modify the do-file without breaking the existing structure? A good result preserves the original format while adding the new columns and missing value logic.

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true" appearance="simple"}
## Reveal: Strong Output Characteristics

- Clean header with project metadata
- Path globals instead of hard-coded paths
- A loop over outcome variables (avoids repetitive code)
- `eststo` and `esttab` used together
- Missing values handled with `if !inlist(var, .d, .r)`
- Output saved as `.tex` and `.csv`
- No `capture` commands
- Comments explain *why*, not just *what*
:::

---

# Quick Reference {#sec-reference}

## Common Interactions

| What You Want | What to Type |
|---------------|-------------|
| Start Claude Code | `claude` in the terminal, inside your project folder |
| Ask a question | Type at the `>` prompt |
| Edit a file | *"Edit [path] to [change]"* |
| Create a file | *"Create a do-file that [spec]"* |
| Run Stata | *"Run `just stata-script mel/2_AttritionAnalysis`"* |
| Debug an error | Paste the log and ask *"What is wrong?"* |
| Exit | `/exit` or `Ctrl+C` |

## Safe vs. Unsafe

::::: {.grid}
:::: {.g-col-6}
:::{.card .card-safe}
### ✅ Safe to Ask
- "Write a do-file that..."
- "Debug this log output..."
- "Explain this Stata command..."
- "Generate a LaTeX table for..."
- "Review this do-file for errors..."
:::
::::
:::: {.g-col-6}
:::{.card .card-danger}
### ❌ Never Ask
- "Read the raw survey data..."
- "Show me observations where name =..."
- "Summarize the PII variables..."
- "List all respondent names..."
- "Open the file on D: drive..."
:::
::::
:::::

## Key Files in Your Project

| File | Contents |
|------|---------|
| `CLAUDE.md` | Project overview, rules, pipeline map |
| `documentation/CLAUDE_STATA_GUIDE.md` | How Claude Code runs Stata |
| `documentation/CLAUDE_DELIVERABLES_GUIDE.md` | Table formatting, LaTeX pipeline |
| `.claude/skills/stata/SKILL.md` | IPA/DIME Stata coding standards |

---

# Next Steps {#sec-next}

Start small and build up. Good first tasks after completing this guide:

::::: {.grid}
:::: {.g-col-6}
:::{.card}
### 🟢 Start Here
Ask Claude Code to **explain** an unfamiliar section of an existing do-file.
:::
::::
:::: {.g-col-6}
:::{.card}
### 🔵 Then Try
Paste a **Stata error** from a log and ask for a diagnosis.
:::
::::
:::: {.g-col-6}
:::{.card}
### 🟣 Build Up
Ask Claude Code to **review** a do-file you wrote for coding standard violations.
:::
::::
:::: {.g-col-6}
:::{.card}
### 🟠 Go Further
Generate a **summary statistics table** for a presentation or report.
:::
::::
:::::

**Resources:**

- [Claude Code documentation](https://docs.anthropic.com/en/docs/claude-code/overview) for the full feature set
- Your project's `CLAUDE.md` for project-specific conventions
- The `.claude/skills/` directory for domain knowledge available to Claude Code

::: {.callout-note appearance="simple"}
Claude Code is a tool, not a replacement for analytical judgment. Always review the code it generates, verify statistical choices, and confirm outputs before sharing results.
:::
